focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The calibration room held six workstations. Only four were in use. Nick noticed this on his first morning in calibration — Monday, September 18, Oakland fog still pressed against the business park windows at nine-thirty. The empty stations jumped out before he'd even decided to look, the old compositional reflex still firing. The room was at the end of the second-floor hallway, past the bathrooms and the supply closet, behind a frosted-glass door with a card reader. He'd walked past it for seven months without registering it. Most days it had been as ignorable as a wall; you didn't think about the wiring behind the drywall.

Inside, it was quieter than the main floor by a factor that felt physical, like altitude. No cascade of Slack chimes, no murmur of forty people clicking through batches in parallel. The overhead fluorescents were the same — nobody had upgraded anything — but with fewer bodies the room ran cooler, the HVAC pushing treated air that smelled identical to every other room in the building. The carpet was the same beige. The monitors were bigger. Dual thirty-two-inch displays instead of the twenty-fours on the evaluation floor, which meant the images loaded at higher resolution, which meant you saw more.

This was either the point or the problem.

The other three members of the San Leandro calibration team were already at their stations when Nick sat down. He'd been introduced by Marcus during a transition meeting the previous Friday — a Zoom call from San Francisco where Marcus used phrases like "calibration integrity" and "trust architecture" with the fluency of someone who'd been saying them long enough that they'd stopped sounding like jargon and started sounding like belief. The in-person introductions were briefer. Glen, mid-forties, fo...
open_hooks_to_carry: Marcus visiting the San Leandro floor — his charisma and genuine belief in the work
The calibration-team offer and Nick's suffocating awareness of the irony
Dani being passed over — her silence in the break room, the coffee mug she washes without looking at Nick
Nick's awareness that his promotion cost Dani something she needed more

payoffs_to_land.description: Nick receives the access necessary to execute the method discovered in Ch 8. The promotion is the key.

prev_tail_excerpt:
...Nick felt the answer catch and die in his throat before it was even language. He worried the X-Acto scar on his right index finger with his thumb through his pocket, the old habit when he needed to keep still. The lot hummed around them. He had the stupid clear sense that if he said the wrong thing now the whole night would tilt toward it.

They stopped beside the driver's door. Dani's keys rested in her palm. Nick's hands stayed in his pockets. The red glow from the neon sign cut across her mouth, and a strip of blue from the nail salon caught in her hair. She was looking at him.

He looked back longer than he meant to. Long enough to see her swallow. Long enough that the hum of the strip mall dropped behind the blood in his ears. Heat came up through the soles of his shoes. He could see the place where the red light stopped and her skin began.

Dani held his eyes for one more beat, then thumbed the key fob. The Honda chirped. She took half a step back and pulled the driver's door open.

"OK," she said.

"Goodnight," Nick said.

"Night."

She got in. The engine started. She backed out and pulled toward the lot's exit. He watched the taillights turn left toward the freeway on-ramp and disappear.

He walked to his Civic. The car was an oven, baked all day in the July sun, and he rolled the windows down before sitting behind the wheel. Rosie's red neon smeared across the windshield. His key turned. As he backed out of the lot, the night came back in pieces: Bao tucking the five under his glass with two fingers, Priya saying one bad call didn't stay one bad call in a place like this, Tommy's voice going high when he finally won, Dani's green-marker horse taped crooked in the rear window. He drove home with the windows down, and for the length of the drive, that was enough.
secondary_character_beats: Marcus must register as a real person with real convictions, not a corporate villain. His belief in the human layer is sincere, which makes Nick's betrayal of his trust worse
Dani's non-reaction is the loudest thing in the chapter — she doesn't complain because she can't afford to, and that restraint should be legible as economic constraint, not nobility
state_deltas_required: Marcus offers Nick the calibration-team position — exactly what Nick needs — while Dani is passed over for the same role
Nick gets the keys to the system; the cost is Dani's professional loss and a fracture in their friendship that neither acknowledges aloud
